name: Adrianna Catena
bio: Fourth-generation leader of one of Argentina's most internationally established wine families. Trained as a geologist with degrees from Harvard and Stanford before assuming a leading role at the family estate. Has overseen the systematic mapping and classification of microterroirs in the Uco Valley, particularly at the high-altitude Adrianna Vineyard at 1,500 metres in Gualtallary. The estate's single-parcel bottlings — Mundus Bacillus Terrae, Fortuna Terrae, River Stones — emerge from this work. The broader Catena Zapata portfolio spans Malbec, Chardonnay, and Cabernet Franc across multiple altitudes and soil types in Mendoza.

name: Alejandro Vigil
bio: Spent over a decade as head winemaker at Catena Zapata before founding his own projects, El Enemigo and Gran Enemigo. The Gran Enemigo bottlings are single-vineyard Cabernet Francs from Gualtallary, Agrelo, and Chacayes — a variety Vigil has been central in establishing as a serious Mendoza grape. The broader portfolio includes Bonarda, Malbec, and Chardonnay. Vigil continues to consult and collaborate widely across the Argentine wine industry and has been particularly active in advocacy for single-vineyard bottlings as a category in a country historically dominated by blends.

name: Bernhard Ott
bio: Fourth-generation proprietor of Weingut Ott in Feuersbrunn, where the family has farmed since 1889. Took over the cellar in the early 1990s after training at Klosterneuburg. Converted the estate to biodynamic farming in 2006, making Ott one of the earliest and most consistent biodynamic advocates in Austria. Has played a leading role in elevating the Wagram — long overshadowed by the Wachau — as a serious fine wine region. The estate works approximately fifty hectares, almost entirely Grüner Veltliner, with a portfolio organised by site specificity: Am Berg, Fass 4, and the single-vineyard Rosenberg and Stein.

name: Lucas Pichler
bio: Represents the third generation at F.X. Pichler in Oberloiben, working alongside his wife Johanna. The estate holds parcels on Kellerberg, Loibenberg, and Dürnsteiner Liebenberg — steep terraced sites of gneiss, mica schist, and loess. Beginning with the 2020 vintage, Lucas has moved the estate toward earlier picking and more moderate alcohol levels, modifying the muscular style for which the family had been known. The portfolio is built around dry Riesling and Grüner Veltliner; the M (Monumental) designation is reserved for the most exceptional lots in a given vintage.

name: Robert Bodenstein
bio: Next generation at Weingut Prager, gradually assuming winemaking responsibilities from his father Toni Bodenstein. Wrote his master's thesis on the terroir of the Achleiten vineyard. The estate farms 18.5 hectares across steep terraces in Weißenkirchen (Steinriegl, Zwerithaler, Klaus, Achleiten) and Dürnstein (Kaiserberg, Liebenberg), planted to roughly 60% Riesling and 40% Grüner Veltliner. The cellar approach has been resolutely minimalist since 1996 — spontaneous fermentation, no malolactic, exclusively stainless-steel aging. Robert assumed lead winemaking in 2019.

name: Armin Tement
bio: Third-generation winemaker at Weingut Tement in Berghausen, on the Austrian-Slovenian border. Joined the family estate in 2005 and took control of the cellar in 2008. Has shifted the estate toward greater precision and restraint — lower extraction, cooler picking, minimal sulfur — building on the international reputation his father Manfred established beginning with the 1991 vintage. Oversaw the conversion to organic (certified 2015) and biodynamic farming (Demeter and Respekt certified from 2022, making Tement Austria's largest Demeter-certified winery). The estate works approximately one hundred hectares across Austria and Slovenia, principally Sauvignon Blanc and Morillon, with the Zieregg as its flagship grand cru site.

name: Louis-Benoît Desvignes
bio: Eighth generation in Morgon, working at the family estate alongside his sister Claude-Emmanuelle. The domaine's holdings are concentrated in Morgon's two most historically significant lieux-dits — the volcanic Côte du Py and the sandier Javernières. Desvignes uses traditional vinification rather than the semi-carbonic maceration that dominates commercial Beaujolais: destemmed grapes, indigenous yeasts, aging in large old oak. The resulting wines are among the most age-worthy in Beaujolais, departing from the early-drinking style typical of the region.

name: Guillaume Pouthier
bio: Winemaker at Château Les Carmes Haut-Brion in Pessac-Léognan since 2012. Has introduced techniques unusual in Bordeaux — whole-cluster fermentation, extended cold maceration, gentler extraction — drawing more from Burgundy and the Northern Rhône than from Bordeaux convention. The estate's unusual urban location within Bordeaux city limits, the high proportion of Cabernet Franc (around 40%) in the blend, and Pouthier's willingness to depart from regional orthodoxy have shifted the estate's stylistic positioning over the past decade.

name: Jean-Nicolas Méo
bio: Proprietor of Domaine Méo-Camuzet in Vosne-Romanée, taking over in 1989 with limited winemaking experience and learning largely from Henri Jayer, who was the family's former tenant farmer. The domaine's portfolio includes Cros Parantoux (the vineyard Jayer made famous), Richebourg, Corton, and a number of premier crus across the Côte de Nuits. The estate style — dark-fruited, layered, and silky — bears Jayer's imprint, though the new-oak signature has become more restrained over recent decades. Méo has expanded into the Jura, Oregon, and Fixin under separate labels.

name: Cyril Chirouze
bio: Steward of Clos Rougeard in Saumur-Champigny following the deaths of the Foucault brothers, Charly and Nady. Apprenticed under the Foucaults for years before assuming responsibility, absorbing their methodology: old-vine Cabernet Franc from tuffeau limestone caves, native yeast fermentation, aging in used Burgundy barrels, bottling without filtration. The top cuvées — Les Poyeux and Le Bourg — are sourced from the estate's oldest parcels and bottled in tiny quantities. The estate has remained closely held, with allocations tightly controlled.

name: Stéphane Ogier
bio: Took over the family domaine in Ampuis from his father Michel in 1997. Has refined the estate style toward whole-cluster fermentation, gentler extraction, and a textural emphasis closer to Burgundy than to traditional Côte-Rôtie. The portfolio is unusually broad: an entry-level Côtes du Rhône (Le Temps est Venu), Côte-Rôtie from individual lieux-dits including Lancement and Montmain, the single-vineyard La Belle Hélène from a steep mica-schist parcel, plus Condrieu, Saint-Joseph, and Viognier IGP bottlings.

name: Cornelius Dönnhoff
bio: Represents the current generation at the family estate in Oberhausen, having taken over winemaking from his father Helmut in the early 2010s. The domaine's holdings include Hermannshöhle (porphyry and slate), Niederhäuser Brücke, Felsenberg Felsentürmchen, and Oberhäuser Leistenberg. Winemaking is non-interventionist: spontaneous fermentation, neutral casks, long lees contact, minimal sulfur. The portfolio spans dry GG bottlings, off-dry styles, and sweet Auslese, BA, and TBA wines in years when conditions allow.

name: Sebastian Fürst
bio: Tenth-generation winemaker at the family estate in Bürgstadt, which has been making wine since 1638. Assumed control in 2018 from his father Paul. Trained at Geisenheim and completed extended stints in Burgundy, Alsace, Spain, and South Africa. The 21-hectare estate is planted predominantly to Spätburgunder (around 60%) on red Buntsandstein with clay and loam overlays. The GG bottlings come from Hundsrück, Centgrafenberg, and the Schlossberg monopole in Klingenberg. The Centgrafenberg Riesling GG is the estate's principal white. Winemaking is Burgundy-influenced: high whole-cluster proportions, basket pressing, 13–18 months in barriques.

name: Hans Rebholz
bio: Next generation at the family estate in Siebeldingen, gradually assuming responsibility alongside his twin brother Valentin from their father Hansjörg. The twins apprenticed at Domaine Jean-Marc Roulot and Frédéric Magnien in Burgundy and at Bodega Chacra in Patagonia. The estate's 26 biodynamically farmed hectares span three terroirs: the limestone Im Sonnenschein monopole, the red-slate Kastanienbusch (source of one of Germany's significant dry Riesling GGs), and the sandstone Ganzhorn. The family's longstanding 'Naturweingedanke' philosophy — fully dry wines with no chaptalisation or sweet reserve — was articulated by Hans's great-grandfather Eduard and remains the estate's defining commitment.

name: Johannes Selbach
bio: Runs the family estate in Zeltingen with his son Sebastian gradually taking on responsibility. The domaine farms parcels in some of the Mosel's steepest slate vineyards, including Zeltinger Sonnenuhr, Zeltinger Himmelreich, and parcels in Wehlen, Bernkastel, and Graach. The portfolio covers the full Prädikat spectrum from dry Kabinett to BA and TBA, with the Sonnenuhr Spätlese and Auslese as central reference points. The Alte Reben bottlings come from ungrafted old-vine parcels. Selbach has been one of the Mosel's most active international advocates over the past three decades.

name: Robert Weil
bio: Fifth generation at the family estate in Kiedrich, founded in 1875 by his great-great-grandfather Dr. Robert Weil. Has assumed responsibility from his father Wilhelm, the great-grandson of the founder, who reshaped the estate into its modern form. The estate's principal site is the Kiedrich Gräfenberg, a steep southwest-facing amphitheatre of phyllite slate and loess that was classified as a first-class site in 1867. The portfolio is built around Riesling across the full Prädikat spectrum, from dry GG bottlings to TBA. The estate has long been associated with both the dry and sweet Riesling traditions of the Rheingau.

name: Christos Zafeirakis
bio: Founded the family estate in Tyrnavos, Thessaly, in 1998, returning to a region historically associated with ouzo production rather than fine wine. Has worked extensively with the Limniona grape, an indigenous variety that had nearly disappeared, sourcing from old and in some cases ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines. Farming is biodynamic. The cellar approach is minimalist: native yeasts, old oak, low sulfur. The portfolio also includes Malagousia (white) and Syrah. Zafeirakis has been active in advocacy for Greek indigenous varieties beyond the established Assyrtiko and Xinomavro.

name: Giorgio Rivetti
bio: Took over the family estate in the late 1970s, when La Spinetta was a Moscato d'Asti producer, and shifted its focus toward single-vineyard Barbaresco beginning in the 1990s. The principal Barbaresco bottlings come from three crus: Starderi, Valeirano, and Gallina. The estate later expanded into Barolo (Campè, from Grinzane Cavour) and into Tuscany with separate projects. The winemaking style — extended maceration, lavish new-oak aging, full extraction — positioned La Spinetta in the modernist camp during Piedmont's traditionalist-modernist debates of the 1990s and 2000s. The Rhinoceros label, an homage to Albrecht Dürer, is the estate's signature.

name: Lorenza Sebasti
bio: CEO of Castello di Ama in Gaiole in Chianti, working alongside her husband, the winemaker Marco Pallanti. Has integrated contemporary art into the estate's identity since the 1990s, commissioning site-specific installations from Anish Kapoor, Daniel Buren, Michelangelo Pistoletto, Louise Bourgeois, and others, distributed across the vineyards and cellars. The Chianti Classico Gran Selezione bottlings — San Lorenzo, Bellavista, La Casuccia — come from single vineyards in the high-altitude commune of Gaiole. L'Apparita, a pure Merlot from a single parcel, is the estate's flagship varietal bottling outside the Sangiovese range.

name: Federico Radi
bio: Commercial director at the historic Tenuta Greppo in Montalcino, the estate where Ferruccio Biondi-Santi codified what became Brunello di Montalcino in the late 19th century. Following the 2016 acquisition by the EPI group (the French Descours family), the estate has maintained its traditional approach: extended maceration, extended aging in large Slavonian oak (up to four years for the Riserva), and a rigorous selection process that yields only two wines — the Annata and, in exceptional vintages, the Riserva. Radi serves as the estate's principal public advocate and has been a vocal defender of its hyper-traditional posture.

name: Giovanni Manetti
bio: Proprietor of Fontodi in Panzano in Chianti and President of the Consorzio Chianti Classico. The estate sits in the Conca d'Oro, a natural amphitheatre of south-facing slopes on galestro and alberese soils. Farming is fully organic. The Chianti Classico Gran Selezione Vigna del Sorbo and the Flaccianello della Pieve — a pure Sangiovese IGT first bottled in 1981, predating the broader super-Tuscan category — anchor the portfolio. Manetti has been particularly active in advocating for the Gran Selezione classification and for Sangiovese as a vehicle for site expression.

name: Benjamin Franchetti
bio: Carries forward the estate his father Andrea Franchetti founded in the Val d'Orcia in 1990, planting Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Petit Verdot on iron-rich clay slopes at around 500 metres in a previously uncultivated area. The flagship Trinoro is predominantly Cabernet Franc, made at very low yields. The family also established Passopisciaro on Mount Etna, pioneering the single-contrada concept with Nerello Mascalese, and Château Petrogano in Bordeaux. Benjamin has been gradually assuming responsibility for the Trinoro estate following his father's death in 2024.

name: Marco Dal Forno
bio: Proprietor and winemaker at the estate in Illasi, on the eastern edge of the Valpolicella denomination. Trained alongside Giuseppe Quintarelli in his early career, then developed an extreme version of the appassimento technique — drying grapes for up to three months in a purpose-built temperature-controlled facility — and built the estate's gravity-flow winery accordingly. Production focuses on Amarone and Valpolicella Superiore, both aged for extended periods in new French and Slavonian oak barriques. Output is small, allocations are tightly held, and the estate operates largely outside the broader Valpolicella industry.

name: Blair Walter
bio: Has been head winemaker at Felton Road since the estate's founding in the late 1990s. The estate is located in Bannockburn, a sun-drenched subregion of Central Otago with a continental climate unusual for New Zealand. Farming has been biodynamic since the early 2000s. The single-block Pinot Noir bottlings — Block 3, Block 5, Calvert, and Cornish Point — express different parcels of the estate's north-facing slopes. The portfolio also includes Chardonnay and dry Riesling. Walter is one of New Zealand's longest-tenured single-estate winemakers.

name: Evan Martin
bio: Founded Martin Woods in the McMinnville AVA of the Willamette Valley in 2010, working as a small-production natural winemaker. The cellar approach is hands-off: indigenous yeasts, no fining or filtration, modest sulfur additions. The portfolio centres on Gamay, Pinot Noir, and Riesling, sourced from volcanic and sedimentary soils across the northern Willamette Valley. The Gamay bottlings have been particularly central to the estate's identity, positioning Martin Woods within the broader natural-wine conversation in Oregon alongside producers such as Statera Cellars and Bow & Arrow.

name: Ana Terčič
bio: Heads a small family estate in San Floriano del Collio, on the Italian side of the cross-border wine region that includes both the Italian Collio and the Slovenian Goriška Brda. The Terčič family's Slovenian roots place the estate at the cultural and linguistic intersection of two winemaking traditions on a continuous geological unit of opoka marl and flysch. The portfolio focuses on local whites: Ribolla Gialla, Friulano, Pinot Grigio, and Malvasia. Winemaking is restrained, with emphasis on the textural depth that opoka soils provide.

name: Andrea Mullineux
bio: American-born, trained at UC Davis and Stellenbosch. Co-founded Mullineux Family Wines in the Swartland in 2007 with her husband Chris. The estate has been central to the Swartland's emergence as a fine wine region over the past two decades, alongside producers such as Eben Sadie and David Sadie. The core portfolio is built around Syrah, Chenin Blanc, and the old-vine Straw Wine. The Schist and Iron single-terroir Syrah bottlings express two distinct soil types within the region — schist and iron-rich gravel — from vineyards farmed by long-term partners.

name: Raúl Pérez
bio: A consulting and own-label winemaker working across multiple regions of northwestern Spain — Bierzo, Galicia, Tierra de León, Rías Baixas, and others. Pérez was formerly head winemaker at Castro Ventosa, his family's estate in Bierzo, before launching his own micro-cuvée projects. The portfolio is unusually broad: Sketch (a skin-contact Albariño), the Ultreia series (single-vineyard Mencía from Bierzo), and the La Vizcaína bottlings from centenarian vines in Valtuille de Abajo, among many others. Pérez has been particularly active in restoring forgotten or marginal vineyard sites across northwestern Spain.

name: Pepe Raventós
bio: Heads the family estate in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, where Raventós ancestors have been making wine since 1497. In 2012, withdrew the estate from the Cava DO and established the Conca del Riu Anoia classification — a self-imposed framework for serious traditional-method sparkling wine from a defined estate-and-river geography. The estate works indigenous Xarel·lo, Macabeo, and Parellada, plus some Monastrell for rosé. Farming is fully organic and biodynamic. Wines undergo extended lees aging; the principal bottlings are Manuel Raventós Negra and Textures de Pedra.

name: Maya Dalla Valle
bio: Proprietor of the family estate on the eastern slopes of Oakville, founded by her parents Naoko and Gustav Dalla Valle in the 1980s. The estate sits on volcanic and sedimentary hillside soils above the valley floor. The flagship Maya — named for her — is a Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant blend with Cabernet Franc. Maya has assumed leadership of the estate following her father's death and has maintained the low-intervention farming and stylistic restraint the estate has been known for, working alongside consulting winemaker Andy Erickson.

name: Chris Peterson
bio: Founded Avennia in 2010 after working at DeLille Cellars, sourcing from established Washington vineyards across Red Mountain, Walla Walla Valley, and Horse Heaven Hills. Works from Woodinville, the warehouse winery district north of Seattle. The Bordeaux blends — Justine and Sestina — and the Arnaut Syrah anchor the portfolio. The approach pairs Washington fruit with a Bordeaux-influenced emphasis on structure, acidity, and age-worthiness. Peterson is among the Washington producers most associated with structured, restrained styles in a state more often known for fruit-driven wines.

name: Austin Peterson
bio: Winemaker at Ovid, a small estate on Pritchard Hill east of Rutherford at around 1,400 feet elevation. The estate was founded by Mark and Dana Nelson in the early 2000s and was acquired by Duckhorn Wine Company in 2017. The site's volcanic red soils and elevation produce structured Cabernet-dominant blends. The flagship Ovid is typically a blend of the five Bordeaux varieties; the Hexameter is Cabernet Franc-forward; the Experiment series explores varied vinification approaches. Peterson works alongside consulting winemaker Michel Rolland.

name: Matt Dees
bio: Head winemaker at both The Hilt and Jonata in Santa Barbara County, two estates established by the late Stan Kroenke. At The Hilt, in the Sta. Rita Hills, works with Pinot Noir and Chardonnay on cool, wind-exposed marine sites. At Jonata, in Ballard Canyon, works with Syrah (Todos), Cabernet Sauvignon, Sangiovese, and Sauvignon Blanc on warmer Mediterranean ground. Dees took over both estates in the early 2010s and has shaped them in distinct stylistic directions appropriate to their differing climates, working closely with the long-term vineyard teams at each site.

name: Mark Williams
bio: Head winemaker at Denner in the Willow Creek district of Paso Robles, a limestone-rich subregion at around 1,700 feet elevation. The estate was founded in the late 1990s by Ron Denner. Williams works principally with Rhône varieties: the Dirt Worshipper (Grenache-Mourvèdre-Syrah) and Ditch Digger (Grenache Blanc-Roussanne) are the principal bottlings, along with Bordeaux blends and Cabernet Sauvignon. The winemaking emphasises moderate extraction and judicious oak, in contrast to the heavier styles often associated with Paso Robles.

name: Raj Parr
estate: Phelan Farm / Domaine de la Côte / Sandhi
bio: A former sommelier who transitioned to winemaking in the early 2000s. Co-founded Sandhi (Chardonnay) in 2010 and Domaine de la Côte (Pinot Noir) in the western Sta. Rita Hills, in both cases in partnership with Sashi Moorman. Most recently founded Phelan Farm in the Santa Ynez Valley, where the focus shifts toward skin-contact whites, Trousseau, and other less-conventional varieties. Parr is also the author of The Sommelier's Atlas of Taste (with Jordan Mackay) and has been an active public advocate for Burgundian and Jurassien wines through his writing and speaking.
